name_original: Capitania General de las Islas Filipinas
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Spanish colonial Philippines was administered as part of the Viceroyalty of New Spain (Mexico) until 1821, then directly from Madrid. Spanish colonization imposed Catholicism, the encomienda system, and the reduccion policy that forcibly resettled indigenous communities into compact towns for taxation and religious conversion. The Moro Wars (1565-1898) against the Muslim south represent one of the longest colonial conflicts in history. Indigenous pre-colonial polities (barangay, rajahnates, sultanates) were systematically dismantled. The Galleon Trade (1565-1815) connected Manila to Acapulco but enriched Spain while exploiting Filipino labor. The Philippine Revolution of 1896-1898 under Andres Bonifacio and Emilio Aguinaldo was a genuine independence movement subsequently betrayed by US annexation.

name_variants.name: Spanish East Indies
name_variants.context: English general designation for Spanish colonial possessions in Southeast Asia

name_variants.name: Las Islas Filipinas
name_variants.context: Named after King Philip II of Spain

territory_changes.description: Miguel Lopez de Legazpi established the first permanent Spanish settlement in Cebu, beginning formal colonization of the Philippine archipelago. Indigenous barangay political structures were progressively dismantled and replaced with Spanish colonial administration.

Legazpi conquered Manila from Rajah Sulayman and the Islamic Rajahnate of Maynila, establishing it as the colonial capital. The strategic harbor became the terminus of the Manila-Acapulco Galleon Trade, transforming the city into a major entrepot.

Following the Philippine Revolution (1896-1898) and the Spanish-American War, Spain ceded the Philippines to the United States via the Treaty of Paris for $20 million. Filipino revolutionaries, who had declared independence on June 12, 1898, were excluded from negotiations. The subsequent Philippine-American War (1899-1902) caused an estimated 200,000-1,000,000 Filipino civilian deaths.

sources.citation: Phelan, John Leddy. The Hispanization of the Philippines: Spanish Aims and Filipino Responses, 1565-1700. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1959
Francia, Luis H. A History of the Philippines: From Indios Bravos to Filipinos. New York: Overlook Press, 2010
Schurz, William Lytle. The Manila Galleon. New York: E.P. Dutton, 1939

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The twin kingdoms of Gowa and Tallo (often treated as a single polity, 'Makassar') were the dominant maritime power of eastern Indonesia before Dutch conquest. Sultan Hasanuddin's resistance to VOC monopoly earned him the epithet 'Cock of the East' from the Dutch. The Treaty of Bungaya (1669), imposed by the VOC with Bugis allies, was a profoundly unequal treaty that destroyed Makassarese sovereignty and trade. The VOC systematically dismantled Makassar's role as a free-trade port, forcing spice trade through Dutch monopoly channels. Makassarese diaspora communities spread across the archipelago as a result of the conquest.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Makassar
name_variants.context: Common English designation for the Gowa-Tallo dual polity

territory_changes.description: Under Karaeng Tumapa'risi Kallonna, Gowa began expanding from a small agricultural chiefdom to dominate neighboring polities in South Sulawesi. Alliance with Tallo created the dual kingdom structure that characterized Makassarese political organization.

Conversion to Islam under Sultan Alauddin. Gowa-Tallo launched the Islamization wars, forcibly converting Bugis states and extending hegemony across South Sulawesi. Makassar became the largest free-trade port in eastern Indonesia, attracting Malay, Portuguese, English, and Danish traders.

The VOC, allied with the Bugis prince Arung Palakka of Bone, defeated Sultan Hasanuddin after prolonged warfare. The Treaty of Bungaya stripped Gowa of sovereignty, imposed trade monopolies, and installed a permanent Dutch fort (Fort Rotterdam). Makassar's role as an independent trading center was destroyed.

sources.citation: Andaya, Leonard Y. The Heritage of Arung Palakka: A History of South Sulawesi (Celebes) in the Seventeenth Century. The Hague: Martinus Nijhoff, 1981
Reid, Anthony. Southeast Asia in the Age of Commerce, 1450-1680. 2 vols. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1988-1993
Cummings, William. Making Blood White: Historical Transformations in Early Modern Makassar. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 2002

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Tidore was Ternate's principal rival in the Spice Islands. The sultanate allied with Spain and Portugal against Ternate's Dutch-allied strategy, demonstrating that indigenous polities actively played European powers against each other. Tidore's territorial claims extended to parts of Halmahera, coastal Papua, and the Raja Ampat Islands -- making it one of the largest pre-colonial polities in eastern Indonesia. The rivalry between Ternate and Tidore predates European arrival and should not be reduced to a proxy conflict between European powers.

territory_changes.description: Traditional founding date of the Tidore polity. Early Tidore was a clove-producing chiefdom that gradually expanded its influence across neighboring islands. The exact founding date is semi-legendary.

Magellan's expedition reached Tidore. Sultan Al-Mansur allied with Spain, beginning a strategic partnership against Portuguese-allied Ternate. This alliance brought European military technology but also entangled Tidore in European imperial rivalries.
The Dutch colonial government formally abolished the Sultanate of Tidore's political authority, incorporating its territories into the Netherlands East Indies administrative structure.

sources.citation: Andaya, Leonard Y. The World of Maluku: Eastern Indonesia in the Early Modern Period. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1993
Pigafetta, Antonio. Primo viaggio intorno al mondo. 1525. (Account of Magellan's expedition reaching Tidore)

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sultanate of Johor was the direct successor state of the Sultanate of Malacca, founded by the fleeing Malaccan royal family after the Portuguese conquest of 1511. Johor's history is central to Malay identity and the concept of 'Melayu' (Malayness). The sultanate claimed legitimate succession from Malacca and therefore from Srivijaya, underpinning the idea of a continuous Malay political tradition. Modern Malaysian and Singaporean national narratives draw heavily on Johor's legacy. The Temenggong line that eventually controlled Singapore was a branch of the Johor ruling elite.

territory_changes.region: Southern Malay Peninsula and Riau Islands
territory_changes.description: Sultan Alauddin Riayat Shah II, son of the last Sultan of Malacca, established a new capital at the southern tip of the Malay Peninsula, founding the Sultanate of Johor as successor to Malacca.

Johor allied with the Dutch VOC to capture Malacca from Portugal. While Johor hoped to restore Malay control over the strategic port, the Dutch kept Malacca for themselves, establishing a pattern of European betrayal of indigenous allies.

Temenggong Ibrahim consolidated control over mainland Johor under British protection. The sultanate was effectively split: the Riau-Lingga islands went to the Dutch sphere and mainland Johor to the British sphere under the 1824 Anglo-Dutch Treaty.

sources.citation: Andaya, Leonard Y. The Kingdom of Johor, 1641-1728: Economic and Political Developments. Kuala Lumpur: Oxford University Press, 1975
Tuhfat al-Nafis (Precious Gift). Raja Ali Haji, c. 1866. Malay court chronicle of the Johor-Riau sultanate.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sultanate of Banten was a major independent Islamic trading state in western Java and southern Sumatra. Sultan Ageng Tirtayasa's resistance to VOC monopoly in the 1680s represents one of the most significant episodes of Indonesian anti-colonial resistance. The VOC exploited a succession crisis between Sultan Ageng and his son Sultan Haji to intervene militarily, installing a puppet ruler and imposing trade monopolies. Banten's pepper trade, previously open to all nations, was forcibly monopolized by the Dutch, devastating the local economy. The VOC's destruction of Banten's cosmopolitan trade network is a case study in how European colonialism undermined existing Asian commercial systems.

name_variants.name: Bantam
name_variants.context: European colonial-era name, used in English and Dutch sources

territory_changes.description: Sunan Gunung Jati, one of the Wali Songo (Nine Saints of Javanese Islam), established an Islamic sultanate at Banten after conquering the Hindu-Buddhist port of Sunda Kelapa. Banten quickly became a major pepper-exporting port.

The VOC intervened in Banten's civil war, supporting Sultan Haji against his father Sultan Ageng Tirtayasa. Dutch military victory ended Banten's independence and imposed exclusive trade agreements. Sultan Ageng was imprisoned until his death in 1692.

The British (during the brief British interregnum under Stamford Raffles) abolished the Sultanate of Banten entirely, annexing its territory into direct colonial administration. The last sultan was deposed.

sources.citation: Guillot, Claude, Lukman Nurhakim, and Sonny Wibisono. Banten avant l'Islam: Etude archeologique de Banten Girang. Paris: EFEO, 1994
Ota, Atsushi. Changes of Regime and Social Dynamics in West Java: Society, State and the Outer World of Banten, 1750-1830. Leiden: Brill, 2006

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sunda Kingdom (also called Pajajaran) was a Hindu-Buddhist polity in western Java whose history is underrepresented compared to the Javanese kingdoms of Central and East Java. The Sundanese people maintain a distinct cultural identity from the Javanese, and the history of the Sunda Kingdom is important to Sundanese ethnic consciousness. The fall of Sunda to the Islamic Sultanate of Banten (1579) and the earlier betrayal at Sunda Kelapa by Demak-allied forces (1527) are sensitive events that involved the destruction of Hindu-Buddhist culture in western Java. The Carita Parahyangan, the main Sundanese chronicle, mixes historical and legendary material.

name_variants.name: Pajajaran
name_variants.context: Sundanese name, after the capital Pakuan Pajajaran

name_variants.name: Sunda Galuh
name_variants.context: Earlier designation when united with the eastern Sundanese polity of Galuh

territory_changes.description: The earliest known inscription mentioning 'Sunda' dates to the Kebon Kopi inscription (c. 5th century). By the 7th century, the Sunda polity was consolidated in the highlands of western Java with connections to maritime trade.

The Sunda Kingdom had negotiated a treaty with the Portuguese to establish a trading post at Sunda Kelapa. Before the Portuguese arrived, Demak-allied Muslim forces under Fatahillah conquered the port, renaming it Jayakarta. This cut Sunda off from maritime trade and Portuguese military support.

The Sultanate of Banten conquered the Sunda capital Pakuan Pajajaran, ending the last major Hindu-Buddhist kingdom in Java. The event marks the completion of Islamization in western Java. Sundanese chronicles (Carita Parahyangan) record the fall with grief.

sources.citation: Nastiti, Titi Surti. Prasasti Kawali dan Kebon Kopi II: Keterangan Baru tentang Kerajaan Sunda. Jakarta: Pusat Penelitian Arkeologi Nasional, 2005
Carita Parahyangan. Sundanese chronicle, c. 16th century manuscript.
Tome Pires. Suma Oriental. c. 1515. Portuguese account of Southeast Asian kingdoms.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Ava was the primary Burmese successor state after the fall of Pagan. Its history is intertwined with conflicts with the Shan states and the Mon Kingdom of Hanthawaddy, reflecting the ethnic complexity of pre-colonial Burma. Burmese nationalist historiography often portrays Ava as the legitimate continuation of Pagan-era Burmese civilization, but the kingdom's founders were heavily Shan-influenced, and the strict Burman/Shan ethnic distinction is an oversimplification imposed by later historiography.

name_variants.name: Inwa
name_variants.context: Burmese name, from Pali 'entrance to the lake'

name_variants.name: Ratanapura
name_variants.context: Pali ceremonial name meaning 'City of Gems'

territory_changes.description: Thado Minbya founded the Kingdom of Ava at the confluence of the Irrawaddy and Myitnge rivers, reunifying the Upper Burmese heartland after the fragmentation following Pagan's fall. The new kingdom drew on both Burmese and Shan political traditions.

The Forty Years' War between Ava and the Mon Kingdom of Hanthawaddy (1385-1424) ended inconclusively, establishing a lasting division between Upper Burma (Ava) and Lower Burma (Pegu). This ethnic and political fault line persisted for centuries.
Bayinnaung of the Toungoo Dynasty conquered Ava, incorporating it into the reunified Burmese empire. Ava subsequently served as a secondary capital under various later dynasties.

sources.citation: Lieberman, Victor B. Strange Parallels: Southeast Asia in Global Context, c. 800-1830. 2 vols. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2003-2009
Hmannan Mahayazawindawgyi (Glass Palace Chronicle). Compiled 1829, Burmese royal chronicle.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Hanthawaddy (Pegu) was the primary Mon political entity in Lower Burma. Mon identity and history have been systematically marginalized in Burmese nationalist historiography, which centers Burman ethnic identity. The Mon were culturally influential -- introducing Theravada Buddhism and literacy to the Burmese -- yet were repeatedly conquered and their political autonomy destroyed. The fall of Hanthawaddy in 1539 to Toungoo and again in 1757 to Konbaung (with the destruction of the city of Pegu) involved massacres and forced assimilation. Today the Mon are a minority in Myanmar with limited recognition of their historical contributions.

name_variants.name: Hongsa
name_variants.context: Mon name, from the mythical hamsa (sacred goose) associated with the kingdom

territory_changes.description: After the Mongol invasion destroyed Pagan, Mon leaders established an independent kingdom at Martaban (Mottama), later moving the capital to Pegu (Bago). Wareru, the first king, may have been Shan rather than Mon, reflecting the ethnic fluidity of the period.

Under Queen Shin Sawbu and her successor Dhammazedi, Hanthawaddy experienced its golden age. Dhammazedi reformed the Buddhist sangha and promoted trade with India and Southeast Asia. Pegu became one of the wealthiest cities in mainland Southeast Asia.

King Tabinshwehti of Toungoo conquered Pegu after a prolonged siege, ending Hanthawaddy's independence. The Mon population was subjected to Burman rule. A brief Mon restoration (1740-1757) was crushed by the Konbaung dynasty with extreme violence, including the destruction of the city of Pegu.

sources.citation: Aung-Thwin, Michael and Maitrii Aung-Thwin. A History of Myanmar Since Ancient Times: Traditions and Transformations. London: Reaktion Books, 2012
Razadarit Ayedawbon. Mon chronicle of the wars between Hanthawaddy and Ava, c. 16th century.
Lieberman, Victor B. Burmese Administrative Cycles: Anarchy and Conquest, c. 1580-1760. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 1984

name_original: မြောက်ဦးခေတ်
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Mrauk U (Arakan) was an independent Arakanese/Rakhine Buddhist kingdom with strong ties to the Bengal Sultanate and Mughal India. Arakan's history is highly contested in the context of the modern Rohingya crisis. Mrauk U's rulers used both Buddhist and Islamic titles, employed Muslim courtiers, and minted coins with Arabic inscriptions, reflecting a multicultural polity. The question of whether Muslims in historical Arakan were 'indigenous' or 'settlers' is politically charged and instrumentalized by multiple sides. This database records the documented historical evidence without adjudicating modern ethnic claims.

name_variants.name: Arakan
name_variants.context: European colonial-era name for the region and polity

territory_changes.description: Min Saw Mon established the Mrauk U dynasty with military support from the Bengal Sultanate. The new capital at Mrauk U became a fortified city of canals and temples. Arakanese court culture blended Theravada Buddhism with Islamic influences from Bengal.

Under Min Razagyi and successors, Mrauk U expanded to control Chittagong and parts of southeastern Bengal. Arakanese and Portuguese pirates (known as 'Feringhi') raided Bengal extensively, taking slaves and disrupting Mughal trade.

The Konbaung dynasty of Burma conquered Arakan under King Bodawpaya. The Mahamuni Buddha image was carried to Mandalay. Arakanese resistance was crushed, and many Arakanese (Buddhist and Muslim) fled to British Bengal, creating refugee communities that complicate modern ethnic narratives.

sources.citation: Leider, Jacques P. Le Royaume d'Arakan, Birmanie: Son histoire politique entre le debut du XVe et la fin du XVIIe siecle. Paris: EFEO, 2004
Charney, Michael W. Where Jambudipa and Islamdom Converged: Religious Change and the Emergence of Buddhist Communalism in Early Modern Arakan. PhD Dissertation, University of Michigan, 1999

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sultanate of Patani was an independent Malay Muslim state in what is now southern Thailand. Its forcible incorporation into Siam is the root cause of the ongoing conflict in Thailand's southern border provinces (Pattani, Yala, Narathiwat). Thai nationalist historiography treats Patani as a historically Thai territory, while Malay-Muslim narratives view it as an occupied Malay state. The violent suppression of Patani autonomy, including the 1909 Anglo-Siamese Treaty that placed it under Thai sovereignty without consulting its people, the 1902 abolition of the sultanate, and repeated Thai military crackdowns, are ongoing sources of grievance. This entry records Patani as a historically independent entity per the available evidence.

name_variants.name: Langkasuka
name_variants.context: Possible earlier name of the polity, debated

territory_changes.description: Conversion of the ruling family to Islam and establishment of the sultanate. Patani became a major Islamic center of learning (pondok tradition) and a cosmopolitan trading port attracting Chinese, Japanese, Portuguese, Dutch, and English merchants.

Siam under the Chakri dynasty (Rattanakosin) conquered Patani after the sultanate attempted to assert independence during Siamese political upheaval. The sultanate was divided into seven smaller provinces (Tujuh Hulubalang) to weaken Malay unity.

The Siamese government under Chulalongkorn (Rama V) abolished the semi-autonomous Patani provinces and imposed direct centralized administration, replacing Malay rulers with Thai officials. This was formalized internationally by the 1909 Anglo-Siamese Treaty.

sources.citation: Teeuw, A. and D.K. Wyatt. Hikayat Patani: The Story of Patani. The Hague: Martinus Nijhoff, 1970
Jory, Patrick. A History of Manners and Civility in Thailand. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2016
Che Man, W.K. Muslim Separatism: The Moros of Southern Philippines and the Malays of Southern Thailand. Singapore: Oxford University Press, 1990

name_original: ອານາຈັກວຽງຈັນ
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Vientiane was one of three successor states formed after the breakup of Lan Xang in 1707. Its destruction by Siam in 1828 was one of the most devastating events in Lao history. King Anuvong's revolt against Siamese suzerainty (1826-1828) was met with the complete destruction of Vientiane, deportation of most of its population to Siamese territory, and the near-erasure of the city. Thai nationalist historiography portrays Anuvong as a rebel; Lao nationalist historiography celebrates him as a hero of independence. The depopulation of the Vientiane plain by Siamese forces constitutes a demographic catastrophe whose effects shaped modern Laos.

name_variants.name: เวียงจันทน์

territory_changes.description: Following the succession crisis that split Lan Xang into three kingdoms, Sai Ong Hue established the Kingdom of Vientiane as an independent state controlling the middle Mekong valley. The kingdom initially prospered from trade and Buddhist patronage.

Siamese forces conquered Vientiane, making it a vassal state. The sacred Emerald Buddha (Phra Kaew Morakot) was taken to Bangkok, where it remains today -- a continuing source of Lao cultural grievance.

After King Anuvong's failed revolt against Siamese suzerainty, Siamese forces under Bodin (Singhanat) destroyed Vientiane completely. The population was forcibly deported to Siamese territory. The Vientiane plain was deliberately depopulated. Only Wat Sisaket was left standing.

sources.citation: Stuart-Fox, Martin. The Lao Kingdom of Lan Xang: Rise and Decline. Bangkok: White Lotus Press, 1998
Mayoury Ngaosrivathana and Kennon Breazeale. Breaking New Ground in Lao History: Essays on the Seventh to Twentieth Centuries. Chiang Mai: Silkworm Books, 2002

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Gelgel was the first unified Balinese Hindu kingdom and the primary refuge for Hindu-Javanese culture after the fall of Majapahit. Bali's preservation of Hindu civilization while the rest of the Indonesian archipelago Islamized makes it culturally unique. However, the narrative that Bali 'preserved pure Majapahit culture' is an oversimplification: Balinese Hinduism evolved distinctly, incorporating local animist traditions. The Gelgel period established the caste system (wangsa) and courtly traditions that define Balinese identity today. Dutch colonial scholars romanticized Bali as a 'living museum' of Hindu-Javanese culture, a characterization that served colonial purposes of divide-and-rule.

name_variants.name: Kerajaan Bali
name_variants.context: General Indonesian designation for the Balinese Hindu kingdom

name_variants.name: Dalem Gelgel
name_variants.context: Balinese court designation, 'Dalem' being the title of the ruler

territory_changes.description: Dalem Ketut Ngulesir, traditionally a descendant of Majapahit royalty, consolidated control over Bali and established Gelgel as the paramount court. Mass migration of Hindu-Javanese priests, artisans, and nobles from collapsing Majapahit enriched Balinese culture.

territory_changes.region: Bali, Lombok, and parts of East Java
territory_changes.description: Under Dalem Baturenggong, Gelgel reached its peak, controlling Lombok, Sumbawa, and the easternmost tip of Java (Blambangan). This represented the furthest extent of Hindu political power in the Indonesian archipelago.

Internal rebellions fragmented the Gelgel kingdom. The court relocated to Klungkung, establishing the Dewa Agung as nominal paramount ruler, while regional lords (raja) in Gianyar, Badung, Tabanan, Bangli, Karangasem, Buleleng, and Jembrana became effectively independent.

sources.citation: Vickers, Adrian. Bali: A Paradise Created. Singapore: Periplus Editions, 1989
Babad Dalem. Balinese court chronicle of the Gelgel dynasty.
Creese, Helen. Women of the Kakawin World: Marriage and Sexuality in the Indic Courts of Java and Bali. New York: Routledge, 2004

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sultanate of Cirebon occupied a unique cultural position at the boundary between Sundanese and Javanese cultural zones. Sunan Gunung Jati, its founding figure and one of the Wali Songo, is revered as a saint in Javanese Islamic tradition. Cirebon's syncretic culture blended Javanese, Sundanese, Chinese, and Islamic elements. The VOC forced Cirebon into a subordinate treaty in 1681, beginning the erosion of its sovereignty. The sultanate was eventually split into three competing courts (Kasepuhan, Kanoman, Kacirebonan), a division partly engineered by Dutch colonial policy.

territory_changes.description: Sunan Gunung Jati established Cirebon as an independent Islamic sultanate, strategically located between the Sundanese interior and the Javanese north coast. The port had previously been a vassal of both the Sunda Kingdom and the Sultanate of Demak.

The VOC imposed a treaty on Cirebon during the Trunajaya Rebellion, effectively ending the sultanate's sovereignty. The Dutch subsequently split Cirebon into multiple competing courts to prevent unified resistance.

sources.citation: De Graaf, H.J. and Pigeaud, T.G. De eerste Moslimse vorstendommen op Java: Studien over de staatkundige geschiedenis van de 15de en 16de eeuw. VKI 69, 1974
Babad Cirebon. Javanese court chronicle of the Cirebon sultanate.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Pinya was one of the small successor states that emerged from the collapse of the Pagan Empire. Together with Myinsaing and Sagaing, it represents the fragmentation of Upper Burma in the 14th century. These petty kingdoms are often glossed over in Burmese historical narratives that emphasize the grand empires (Pagan, Toungoo, Konbaung), but they represent an important period of Shan-Burmese cultural interaction. The founding 'Three Shan Brothers' of Myinsaing who preceded Pinya demonstrate the deep Shan influence on Upper Burmese politics.

territory_changes.description: Thihathu, one of the Three Shan Brothers who had taken power at Myinsaing after Pagan's fall, established a new capital at Pinya. The kingdom controlled the Burmese dry zone heartland around the Irrawaddy-Myitnge confluence.
Thado Minbya, a prince with ties to both Pinya and Sagaing, conquered both kingdoms and founded the Kingdom of Ava (Inwa), reunifying Upper Burma.

sources.citation: Hmannan Mahayazawindawgyi (Glass Palace Chronicle). Compiled 1829, Burmese royal chronicle.
Aung-Thwin, Michael. Myth and History in the Historiography of Early Burma. Singapore: ISEAS, 1998

name_original: စစ်ကိုင်းခေတ်
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Sagaing coexisted with Pinya as a rival successor state in Upper Burma. Sagaing is particularly significant for its role as a major center of Theravada Buddhist scholarship and monasticism. The Sagaing Hills remain one of the most important monastic sites in Myanmar. The kingdom's brief independent existence is often overshadowed by the larger polities that preceded and followed it.

territory_changes.description: Saw Yun, a grandson of one of the Three Shan Brothers, established a rival kingdom at Sagaing on the west bank of the Irrawaddy, directly across from Pinya. The two kingdoms coexisted in an uneasy balance.
Sagaing was conquered by the Shan chief Thadomingyi and then quickly absorbed by Thado Minbya, who united it with Pinya to found the Kingdom of Ava.

sources.citation: Hmannan Mahayazawindawgyi (Glass Palace Chronicle). Compiled 1829, Burmese royal chronicle.
Than Tun. The Royal Orders of Burma, A.D. 1598-1885. 10 vols. Kyoto: Center for Southeast Asian Studies, 1983-1990

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sultanate of Palembang Darussalam emerged in the former heartland of Srivijaya and claimed spiritual continuity with that ancient empire. Palembang was a major center of the pepper trade and Chinese-Malay cultural interaction. The Dutch conquered the sultanate in 1821-1823 with considerable violence. Sultan Mahmud Badaruddin II, who resisted the Dutch, is a national hero in Indonesia. The Dutch justification for conquest -- suppression of piracy and protection of trade -- masked the economic exploitation that followed. The large Chinese community in Palembang played a significant economic role but also suffered periodic violence, including a massacre in 1811.

name_variants.name: Palembang Darussalam
name_variants.context: Full Malay name, 'Palembang, Abode of Peace'

territory_changes.description: The Sultanate of Palembang emerged as an independent state after breaking from Mataram's loose suzerainty and Banten's influence. The sultanate controlled the Musi River basin and the lucrative pepper and tin trade of southern Sumatra.

Dutch colonial forces attacked Palembang and deposed Sultan Mahmud Badaruddin II, who had resisted Dutch attempts to monopolize the tin trade and had expelled the Dutch garrison in 1819. The sultan was exiled to Ternate, then Banda.
The Dutch formally abolished the Sultanate of Palembang after suppressing remaining resistance. South Sumatra was incorporated into the Netherlands East Indies as a directly administered residency.

sources.citation: Woelders, M.O. Het Sultanaat Palembang 1811-1825. 's-Gravenhage: Martinus Nijhoff, 1975
Andaya, Barbara Watson. To Live as Brothers: Southeast Sumatra in the Seventeenth and Eighteenth Centuries. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1993

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Bone was the most powerful Bugis state in South Sulawesi. Bugis identity and political tradition are distinct from both Makassarese and Javanese cultures. Arung Palakka, the Bone prince who allied with the VOC to defeat Makassar in 1669, is a highly polarizing figure: celebrated in Bone as a liberator from Makassarese domination, but condemned in Makassarese tradition as a traitor who enabled European colonialism. This database records both perspectives. The Bugis were among the most mobile peoples of the Indonesian archipelago, establishing diaspora communities throughout Southeast Asia.

territory_changes.description: Traditional founding of the Kingdom of Bone by Manurunge ri Matajang, a figure of semi-legendary origin in the Bugis tradition of rulers descending from the heavens (tomanurung). Bone was initially one of many small Bugis chiefdoms.

Arung Palakka of Bone, allied with the VOC, defeated Gowa-Tallo at the Treaty of Bungaya. Bone became the dominant Bugis power in South Sulawesi. However, the VOC alliance gradually transformed into colonial subjugation.

Dutch colonial forces conquered Bone in a series of military expeditions that completed the pacification of South Sulawesi. The last autonomous Bugis rulers were deposed or subordinated to direct colonial administration.

sources.citation: Andaya, Leonard Y. The Heritage of Arung Palakka: A History of South Sulawesi (Celebes) in the Seventeenth Century. The Hague: Martinus Nijhoff, 1981
Pelras, Christian. The Bugis. Oxford: Blackwell, 1996
La Galigo. Bugis epic cycle, one of the longest literary works in the world.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sultanate of Pajang was a transitional state between the decline of Demak and the rise of Mataram, representing the consolidation of Islamic political power in the Javanese interior. Jaka Tingkir (Sultan Hadiwijaya), its founder, is a significant figure in Javanese oral tradition and the Babad Tanah Jawi. His reign marks the shift of Javanese political gravity from the north coast (pasisir) trading cities to the agrarian interior (kejawen), with lasting consequences for Javanese political culture.

territory_changes.description: Jaka Tingkir (later Sultan Hadiwijaya), son-in-law of the last Sultan of Demak, moved the seat of power from the coast to Pajang in the interior. This marked a fundamental shift in Javanese political geography from maritime to agrarian orientation.
Ki Ageng Pemanahan's son Senopati, founder of the Mataram Sultanate, defeated Pajang and established Mataram as the dominant Javanese state. Pajang was absorbed into the Mataram domain.

sources.citation: Ricklefs, M.C. A History of Modern Indonesia Since c. 1200. 4th ed. Basingstoke: Palgrave Macmillan, 2008
Babad Tanah Jawi. Javanese court chronicle, multiple versions from 17th-18th centuries.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sultanate of Banjar in southeastern Borneo controlled the pepper and diamond trades. Its destruction by the Dutch in the Banjar War (1859-1863) involved scorched-earth tactics and the forced abdication of the last sultan. The Banjarese people today maintain a strong Islamic identity rooted in the sultanate period. Dutch colonial exploitation of Borneo's resources (rubber, oil, coal) followed the sultanate's destruction. Prince Antasari's resistance to Dutch conquest is celebrated as a national hero of Indonesia.

name_variants.name: Banjarmasin Sultanate
name_variants.context: Alternative English designation after the capital

territory_changes.description: The Hindu ruler of the Negara Daha kingdom converted to Islam with military aid from Demak, establishing the Sultanate of Banjar. The conversion marked the Islamization of southeastern Borneo and the integration of the region into the wider Malay-Islamic world.

The VOC and later Dutch colonial government imposed increasingly restrictive treaties on Banjar, monopolizing the pepper trade and reducing the sultan's authority. Territorial concessions followed each succession crisis.

The Dutch deposed the last sultan and annexed Banjar territories after the Banjar War (1859-1863). Prince Antasari led fierce guerrilla resistance until his death in 1862. The sultanate was abolished and replaced with direct colonial administration.

sources.citation: Ras, J.J. Hikajat Bandjar: A Study in Malay Historiography. The Hague: Martinus Nijhoff, 1968
Hikayat Banjar. Malay chronicle of the Banjar sultanate.
Lindblad, J. Thomas. Between Dayak and Dutch: The Economic History of Southeast Kalimantan 1880-1942. Dordrecht: Foris, 1988

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Klungkung was the paramount Hindu court of Bali, holding ceremonial precedence (though not always political power) over the other Balinese kingdoms. The Dutch conquest of Bali culminated in the puputan of 1906 (Badung) and 1908 (Klungkung), mass ritual suicides in which Balinese royal families marched into Dutch gunfire rather than surrender. The puputan of Klungkung -- in which Dewa Agung Jambe and his court dressed in white ceremonial garments and walked into Dutch rifle fire -- is one of the most devastating events of Dutch colonialism and profoundly shaped Indonesian national consciousness. Dutch colonial officers expressed shock, yet the conquest continued.

name_variants.name: Semarapura
name_variants.context: Balinese name of the capital, used interchangeably

name_variants.name: Dewa Agung
name_variants.context: Title of the paramount ruler, sometimes used to designate the polity

territory_changes.description: After the collapse of the Gelgel kingdom, the Dewa Agung relocated to Klungkung and established a new capital. Klungkung retained ceremonial supremacy as the 'first among equals' of Balinese kingdoms, but actual political power was distributed among regional courts.
Dutch military expeditions conquered the northern Balinese kingdoms of Buleleng and Jembrana. Klungkung and the southern kingdoms maintained precarious independence under increasing Dutch pressure.

The Dutch attacked Klungkung. Dewa Agung Jambe and approximately 200 members of the royal court performed a puputan -- a ritual mass march into Dutch gunfire. The destruction of the last independent Balinese kingdom completed the Dutch conquest of Bali. The puputan became a symbol of resistance in Indonesian national consciousness.

sources.citation: Wiener, Margaret J. Visible and Invisible Realms: Power, Magic, and Colonial Conquest in Bali. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1995
Vickers, Adrian. Bali: A Paradise Created. Singapore: Periplus Editions, 1989
Robinson, Geoffrey. The Dark Side of Paradise: Political Violence in Bali. Ithaca: Cornell University Press, 1995

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Rajahnate of Maynila was an Islamic polity at the mouth of the Pasig River, predating and directly displaced by Spanish colonization. Rajah Sulayman III resisted Spanish conquest and was killed defending his city. Spanish sources describe Maynila as a thriving Muslim trading settlement with connections to Brunei, Borneo, and the broader Malay-Islamic world. The conversion of Manila's rulers to Islam (from Brunei influence) and the subsequent Spanish re-conquest and Christianization represent a violent erasure of an Islamic layer of Philippine history that Filipino historiography has only recently begun to recover.

name_variants.name: Maynila
name_variants.context: Pre-colonial Tagalog name, possibly from 'may nila' (where the nila/indigo plant grows)

territory_changes.description: Traditional founding of a settlement at the mouth of the Pasig River. The site had been inhabited much earlier, but political consolidation as a distinct polity under a raja likely occurred in the 13th century, with growing influence from Brunei and the Malay world.

Under Bruneian influence, the ruling elite of Maynila converted to Islam. Rajah Matanda and Rajah Sulayman governed a prosperous Muslim trading settlement connected to Brunei, China, and the broader Southeast Asian trade network.

Spanish forces under Miguel Lopez de Legazpi attacked and conquered Maynila. Rajah Sulayman III resisted but was defeated in the Battle of Bangkusay. The Spanish established Manila as their colonial capital, displacing the indigenous political order and beginning forced Christianization.

sources.citation: Scott, William Henry. Barangay: Sixteenth-Century Philippine Culture and Society. Quezon City: Ateneo de Manila University Press, 1994
Pigafetta, Antonio. Primo viaggio intorno al mondo. 1525.
Majul, Cesar Adib. Muslims in the Philippines. Quezon City: University of the Philippines Press, 1973

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sultanate of Sambas in western Borneo was a significant Malay-Islamic polity whose economy was transformed by Chinese gold mining kongsis (communities). The relationship between the Malay sultanate and the Chinese mining settlements was complex -- initially symbiotic but later marked by conflict. The Dutch intervention in Sambas was partly driven by the desire to control Chinese gold mining communities, leading to the destruction of the Lanfang Republic (a Chinese kongsi) in 1884 and the subordination of the sultanate.

territory_changes.description: The Sultanate of Sambas was established on the northwest coast of Borneo, controlling river trade and gold mining areas. The sultanate attracted Chinese miners whose kongsi communities became economically significant.

Dutch colonial forces imposed direct control over Sambas, reducing the sultan to a figurehead. The Dutch sought to control the lucrative gold trade and manage the increasingly powerful Chinese mining kongsis.

sources.citation: Heidhues, Mary Somers. Golddiggers, Farmers, and Traders in the 'Chinese Districts' of West Kalimantan, Indonesia. Ithaca: Cornell Southeast Asia Program, 2003
Irwin, Graham. Nineteenth-Century Borneo: A Study in Diplomatic Rivalry. 's-Gravenhage: Martinus Nijhoff, 1955

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Lanfang Republic was a Chinese-led polity in western Borneo that has been variously described as the 'first republic in Asia' or simply a mining kongsi (corporate community). Chinese nationalist historiography sometimes overstates its political sophistication, while Dutch colonial sources minimized its significance. The reality was a self-governing Hakka Chinese mining community with elected leaders that existed for over a century. Its destruction by the Dutch in 1884 was part of the broader colonial consolidation of Borneo. The Lanfang Republic's multiethnic dimension -- Hakka Chinese coexisting with Dayak and Malay populations -- complicates simplistic ethnic narratives.

name_variants.name: 兰芳公司
name_variants.context: Chinese: 'Lanfang Company', the original kongsi name

territory_changes.description: Lo Fang-pak (Luo Fangbo), a Hakka Chinese, organized the Chinese mining communities of western Borneo into a self-governing polity with elected leaders. The Lanfang kongsi controlled gold mining, trade, and maintained relations with the Sultanate of Pontianak and local Dayak communities.

Dutch colonial forces conquered the Lanfang Republic as part of the consolidation of Netherlands East Indies authority over western Borneo. The last leader (zongting) was deposed and the territory placed under direct colonial administration. Chinese mining communities were subsequently subjected to colonial regulation.

sources.citation: Heidhues, Mary Somers. Golddiggers, Farmers, and Traders in the 'Chinese Districts' of West Kalimantan, Indonesia. Ithaca: Cornell Southeast Asia Program, 2003
Yuan, Bingling. Chinese Democracies: A Study of the Kongsis of West Borneo (1776-1884). Leiden: Research School of Asian, African, and Amerindian Studies, 2000

ethical_notes: ETHICS: British colonization of Burma occurred through three Anglo-Burmese Wars (1824-26, 1852, 1885). The third war and annexation of Upper Burma (1885) involved the exile of King Thibaw and Queen Supayalat, the looting of Mandalay Palace, and the destruction of the Burmese monarchy. British colonial policy favored ethnic minorities (Karen, Chin, Kachin) for military service while marginalizing the Burman majority, creating ethnic divisions that persist in post-independence Myanmar's civil wars. Indian immigration under British rule, particularly into Lower Burma's rice economy, created demographic changes that fueled anti-Indian violence. The colonial extraction of teak, oil, and rice enriched Britain while impoverishing Burmese cultivators.

name_variants.name: Burma Province
name_variants.context: When administered as a province of British India (1886-1937)

territory_changes.description: The Treaty of Yandabo ending the First Anglo-Burmese War ceded Arakan, Tenasserim, Assam, and Manipur to Britain. The Konbaung dynasty was forced to pay a massive indemnity. This was the beginning of the dismemberment of the Burmese empire.

The Third Anglo-Burmese War: Britain invaded Upper Burma on the pretext of trade disputes and French influence. King Thibaw was deposed and exiled to Ratnagiri, India. Mandalay Palace was looted. The Burmese monarchy was abolished. Complete annexation of Burma as a province of British India followed.

Burma gained independence from Britain on January 4, 1948, under the leadership of the Anti-Fascist People's Freedom League. Aung San, the independence leader, had been assassinated in July 1947. The Panglong Agreement (1947) promised autonomy to ethnic minorities, but its implementation remains contested to this day.

sources.citation: Myint-U, Thant. The Making of Modern Burma. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2001
Charney, Michael W. A History of Modern Burma. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2009
Furnivall, J.S. Colonial Policy and Practice: A Comparative Study of Burma and Netherlands India. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1948

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Lombok was a Balinese Hindu kingdom ruling over a predominantly Sasak Muslim population, representing a case of minority ethno-religious rule. The Dutch conquest of Lombok in 1894 was justified as 'liberating' the Sasak from Balinese oppression, but in reality served Dutch imperial interests. The puputan of the Mataram court in Lombok (1894), in which the Balinese raja and his followers chose ritual suicide rather than surrender, was one of the first such events to shock European public opinion. The complex relationship between Balinese Hindu rulers and Sasak Muslim subjects defies simplistic colonizer/colonized narratives.

name_variants.name: Kerajaan Selaparang
name_variants.context: Sasak name, after the earlier indigenous Sasak polity

name_variants.name: Mataram Lombok
name_variants.context: After the Balinese Karangasem dynasty's capital at Mataram (distinct from Javanese Mataram)

territory_changes.description: Balinese forces from the Kingdom of Karangasem conquered western Lombok, establishing a Hindu ruling class over the predominantly Muslim Sasak population. The Balinese gradually extended control over the entire island.

Dutch colonial forces invaded Lombok after Sasak chiefs appealed for intervention against Balinese rule. The siege of Cakranegara ended with the puputan of the Balinese court. The raja's treasury was looted -- the famous 'Lombok treasure' was carried to the Netherlands. Lombok was incorporated into the Netherlands East Indies.

sources.citation: Van der Kraan, Alfons. Lombok: Conquest, Colonization and Underdevelopment, 1870-1940. Singapore: Heinemann, 1980
Vickers, Adrian. Bali: A Paradise Created. Singapore: Periplus Editions, 1989

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sultanate of Pontianak was founded by Sharif Abdurrahman Alkadrie, an Arab-Malay trader from the Hadhrami diaspora, at the exact equator on the coast of western Borneo. The sultanate's founding represents the wider phenomenon of Hadhrami Arab diaspora establishing political authority in the Malay-Indonesian archipelago through trade and Islamic prestige. Pontianak's relationship with the Dutch and the neighboring Chinese kongsis (including the Lanfang Republic) shaped the complex ethnic politics of western Borneo. The Japanese occupation during WWII saw the massacre of the Malay and Chinese elites in the Pontianak Incidents (Mandor Affair, 1943-1944), killing thousands.

territory_changes.description: Sharif Abdurrahman Alkadrie, an Arab-Malay trader of Hadhrami origin, founded Pontianak at the confluence of the Kapuas and Landak rivers on the equator. He consolidated power through alliances with local Dayak and Malay chiefs and by establishing trade relations with the VOC.

Dutch colonial control over Pontianak tightened through a series of treaties that reduced the sultan's authority. The Dutch used Pontianak as a base for operations against the Chinese kongsis of the interior, including the eventual destruction of the Lanfang Republic.
The sultanate was formally abolished during the transition to Indonesian independence. Pontianak became the capital of West Kalimantan province within the Republic of Indonesia.

sources.citation: Heidhues, Mary Somers. Golddiggers, Farmers, and Traders in the 'Chinese Districts' of West Kalimantan, Indonesia. Ithaca: Cornell Southeast Asia Program, 2003
Irwin, Graham. Nineteenth-Century Borneo: A Study in Diplomatic Rivalry. 's-Gravenhage: Martinus Nijhoff, 1955
